summary:
The metallic taste associated with tin-based fluorides arises primarily from the dissolution of stannous hydroxide in saliva, releasing Sn²⁺ ions that stimulate taste receptors. Stannous triphosphate lacks supporting evidence for inducing such sensations, making stannous hydroxide the more plausible source according to current literature. 

(Note: This summary strictly adheres to only clinically significant facts related to mechanism.)

The metallic taste caused by tin-containing compounds results from the release of Sn²⁺ ions when stannous hydroxide dissolves in saliva, stimulating taste receptors. Stannous triphosphate has no documented association with this effect, unlike stannous hydroxide, which reliably releases these ions under physiological conditions.
